metadata.notes: Extracted from the PDF so Codex can inspect the question bank without relying on screenshots or PDF parsing.

cards.title: What Is an LLM?

choices.text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.

choices.text: It reads with conscious understanding, then writes the whole answer at once.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.

choices.text: It searches a hidden database and copies the best matching passage.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Search can be added around a model, but a plain LLM generates from context and learned weights.

choices.text: It changes its weights each time the user sends a new prompt.
choices.feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.

questions.stem: When an LLM answers a prompt by generating one token at a time, what is the model doing at each step?
questions.correct_choice_text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.

choices.text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.

choices.text: It must have inner awareness because the explanation sounds human.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.

choices.text: It must have found a hidden perfect source and copied the interpretation.
choices.feedback: Close, but this describes search or retrieval better than a plain LLM response. A fluent answer is not the same as cited evidence.

choices.text: The poem prompt permanently taught the base model a new skill.
choices.feedback: That would require training. A normal prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.

questions.stem: An LLM gives a fluent explanation of a poem. What should a model-literate learner conclude?
questions.correct_choice_text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.

cards.objective: Explain that an LLM uses learned weights plus current context to score and generate next tokens without implying awareness, search, or permanent learning.

cards.title: Where LLMs Fit

choices.text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.

choices.text: All generative AI tools are LLMs because ChatGPT is generative AI.
choices.feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category.

choices.text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates language tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but image models can be AI without using the LLM text-token loop.

choices.text: AI and machine learning are the same label, so the branches do not matter.
choices.feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI field.

questions.stem: A campus tool summarizes text with an LLM, while another creates images by denoising a noisy pattern. What does that show?
questions.correct_choice_text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.

choices.text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs focus on language or code.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The map matters because an LLM is one branch inside a wider AI landscape.

choices.text: All AI systems are LLMs if they use language.
choices.feedback: That collapses the broad AI family into one model type.

choices.text: Machine learning and AI are exact synonyms.
choices.feedback: That misses that machine learning is a major branch inside broader AI.

choices.text: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same generation loop.
choices.feedback: That treats different generative methods as if they shared one mechanism.

questions.stem: A department uses the words AI, machine learning, generative AI, and LLM as if they mean the same thing. Which correction keeps the category map clear?
questions.correct_choice_text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs focus on language or code.

choices.text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.

choices.text: The whole app, including every tool and button.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The product can include an LLM, but the LLM is not every surrounding tool and screen.

choices.text: The safety policy that blocks some outputs.
choices.feedback: Close, but a safety policy shapes behavior around the model; it is not the language model itself.

choices.text: The database or search index the app may call.
choices.feedback: This describes a retrieval or search component. The LLM generates language from context and learned weights.

questions.stem: A chatbot product includes an LLM, a search tool, safety filters, and a user interface. What is the LLM?
questions.correct_choice_text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.

cards.objective: Use the AI family map to distinguish AI, ML, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, multimodal systems, and AI products.

cards.title: Rationalists vs Empiricists

choices.text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.

choices.text: The rule-based system measures loss and updates weights after every task.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.

choices.text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
choices.feedback: That gives the LLM too much mind. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.

choices.text: There is no difference; both systems are just written rulebooks.
choices.feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.

questions.stem: If a system solves a task only by following hand-written if-then rules, how is it different from an LLM?
questions.correct_choice_text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.

choices.text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.

choices.text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.

choices.text: Loss is the model’s confidence score for the next token during generation.
choices.feedback: Close, but confidence or probability shapes generation. Loss measures training error so weights can be adjusted.

choices.text: Loss writes the final answer after the model has finished thinking.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is used during training; it is not the model writing a response.

questions.stem: During deep-learning training, why does the system measure loss?
questions.correct_choice_text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.

choices.text: The product combines learned patterns, rules, and tools; the LLM inside is still a learned-pattern model.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Modern AI products can mix traditions without making the model itself rule-based.

choices.text: The hand-written checks turn the LLM into symbolic AI.
choices.feedback: That confuses a product wrapper with the model inside it.

choices.text: The retrieved handbook permanently rewrites the model weights.
choices.feedback: Retrieval can add context without becoming durable training.

choices.text: The policy checks prove the model has moral understanding.
choices.feedback: Policy behavior is not the same as conscience or awareness.

questions.stem: A university AI app combines an LLM, hand-written policy checks, and retrieval from a handbook. What should a model-literate user infer?
questions.correct_choice_text: The product combines learned patterns, rules, and tools; the LLM inside is still a learned-pattern model.

cards.objective: Compare rules-first AI and learned-pattern AI while recognizing that modern systems can combine rules, retrieval, policies, tools, and learned models.

cards.title: Training

choices.text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.

choices.text: The model appends the mistaken token to a normal live response.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.

choices.text: The prompt becomes durable memory stored inside the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.

choices.text: The model treats the most likely answer as automatically true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Likelihood is not truth, and this training moment matters because loss can drive a weight update.

questions.stem: During training, the model predicts a target token and gets it wrong. What makes that moment learning rather than ordinary inference?
questions.correct_choice_text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.

choices.text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite the weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: The chat permanently updates the base model’s weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That would require a training process, not ordinary inference.

choices.text: The model stores the prompt as durable memory inside its weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.

choices.text: The optimizer trains the model after every response.
choices.feedback: Close, but optimizers update weights during training, not after every normal response.

questions.stem: A student asks a chatbot one question during ordinary use. Which statement is usually true?
questions.correct_choice_text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite the weights.

choices.text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.

choices.text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights for future runs.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.

choices.text: Training only changes the visible prompt text for one response.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes learned numbers.

choices.text: Training guarantees that every future answer will be true.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.

questions.stem: Why can training change how the model behaves tomorrow, while inference usually cannot?
questions.correct_choice_text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.

choices.text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.

choices.text: Prompt -> response token -> append -> next response token.
choices.feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training.

choices.text: Search -> outside passage -> current context -> answer.
choices.feedback: That describes a search or retrieval step. It can add temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.

choices.text: User correction -> better answer -> permanent learning inside the same chat.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A correction can steer the current conversation, but durable learning requires a training process.

questions.stem: In a training loop, which sequence is the durable learning path?
questions.correct_choice_text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.

cards.objective: Trace training as example, prediction, loss, optimizer, weight update, repeat; separate durable training from inference, retrieval, sampling, and prompt changes.

cards.title: Pretraining

choices.text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.

choices.text: A user conversation at deployment permanently rewrites the model.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Pretraining happens before normal use; an ordinary chat usually does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: A human writes every rule the model will follow.
choices.feedback: Close, but pretraining is learned from examples, not a complete rulebook written by hand.

choices.text: The model stores a perfect copy of every source document.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Pretraining shapes weights; it is not perfect document storage.

questions.stem: During pretraining, what signal teaches the model broad language patterns across many examples?
questions.correct_choice_text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.

choices.text: It shapes statistical patterns in weights; it does not create a searchable copy of every source.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining can shape broad ability without turning the model into a perfect source library.

choices.text: It gives the model direct access to every private file on demand.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Training data exposure is not the same as access to every private file.

choices.text: It inserts the original source into every future prompt context.
choices.feedback: Close, but adding source text to context is different from pretraining. Pretraining changes weights before use.

choices.text: It happens only after a user asks a specific question.
choices.feedback: Pretraining happens before deployment. The user prompt later uses the pretrained weights.

questions.stem: Why can pretraining create broad capability without making the model a perfect library of its sources?
questions.correct_choice_text: It shapes statistical patterns in weights; it does not create a searchable copy of every source.

choices.text: Weights can shift so future prompts activate more useful language/code patterns.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining changes reusable model structure before later prompts arrive.

choices.text: The model’s current chat context becomes permanently larger.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Context is temporary input; pretraining changes reusable weights before later prompts arrive.

choices.text: Only the app’s buttons, labels, or interface change.
choices.feedback: This describes product UI changes, not the model’s learned parameters.

choices.text: Only a safety policy is added around the model.
choices.feedback: Close, but a policy can shape a product around the model; pretraining changes the model’s reusable weights.

questions.stem: If pretraining examples include many styles and task shapes, what can change inside the model?
questions.correct_choice_text: Weights can shift so future prompts activate more useful language/code patterns.

choices.text: Past training shaped weights with broad patterns; the output is not proof of live search or source lookup.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Pretraining can shape fact-like patterns without turning the model into a search engine.

choices.text: The model must have searched the web during the prompt.
choices.feedback: Search is a system/tool action, not something pretraining proves.

choices.text: The model opened a stored copy of the original page.
choices.feedback: Pretraining does not make a perfect searchable library of every source.

choices.text: The model became certain because the text sounded factual.
choices.feedback: Fluent source-like text is not a truth guarantee.

questions.stem: A base model gives fact-like text about a public topic before any retrieval tool is connected. Which pretraining explanation is strongest?
questions.correct_choice_text: Past training shaped weights with broad patterns; the output is not proof of live search or source lookup.

cards.objective: Explain pretraining as broad durable pattern learning from many next-token targets, not perfect recall, live browsing, or a searchable archive.

cards.title: Overfitting and Generalization

choices.text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.
choices.feedback: Good diagnosis. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.

choices.text: Generalization: the model is working well on unseen cases.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Generalization means good performance on new examples; this scenario shows the opposite.

choices.text: Better training: the model has clearly learned the general rule.
choices.feedback: Not quite. If it fails on new validation examples, high training performance is not enough evidence of generalization.

choices.text: Inference: the model is simply generating one token at a time.
choices.feedback: Close, but inference is normal model use; overfitting is a training/evaluation failure mode.

questions.stem: A model gets every training example right but fails on new validation examples. What problem is showing up?
questions.correct_choice_text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.

choices.text: They are kept out of training so they can test whether learned patterns transfer.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.

choices.text: Validation data is just extra training data used to lower loss.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Validation examples are saved for testing, not used as extra examples to fit the model.

choices.text: Training examples alone prove the model will work everywhere.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals the exact overfitting risk. Seen examples are not enough evidence.

choices.text: A lower loss always proves every future answer is true.
choices.feedback: Close, but lower loss is not a truth guarantee, especially outside the tested examples.

questions.stem: Why do model builders test on set-aside validation examples instead of only training examples?
questions.correct_choice_text: They are kept out of training so they can test whether learned patterns transfer.

choices.text: It may have fit the narrow examples too closely instead of learning a pattern that generalizes.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but narrow training can also make behavior brittle.

choices.text: The prompts permanently rewrote the model during inference.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The issue is training behavior, not prompts rewriting weights during inference.

choices.text: The model is definitely conscious but choosing not to answer.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The issue is brittle transfer, not intention.

choices.text: Rule-based AI is always better than learned models.
choices.feedback: This overgeneralizes. The issue is narrow training behavior, not proof that rules always beat learned models.

questions.stem: A model trained heavily on one narrow answer template works on that template but struggles with varied new prompts. What should a model-literate learner suspect?
questions.correct_choice_text: It may have fit the narrow examples too closely instead of learning a pattern that generalizes.

cards.objective: Diagnose overfitting and explain that set-aside validation examples are kept out of training to test transfer beyond examples used to fit weights.

cards.title: Fine-Tuning

choices.text: Run additional training that changes weights or adapter behavior for later responses.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Fine-tuning is durable adaptation for future inference runs.

choices.text: Paste the norms into one user prompt.
choices.feedback: A prompt can steer the current run, but it is not durable training.

choices.text: Retrieve a policy page into the current context.
choices.feedback: Retrieval adds temporary evidence; it does not by itself fine-tune weights.

choices.text: Sample from a lower-temperature distribution.
choices.feedback: Sampling changes a generation-time choice, not the model pattern carried forward.

questions.stem: A team wants a support assistant to follow corporate norms across future conversations. Which move is closest to fine-tuning?
questions.correct_choice_text: Run additional training that changes weights or adapter behavior for later responses.

choices.text: The adapted model’s weights or adapter behavior can carry that pattern into later inference runs.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Fine-tuning makes a reusable model change, not just a one-chat hint.

choices.text: Only the current context window got larger.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A larger context window is temporary input space; fine-tuning changes reusable behavior.

choices.text: The model became conscious of the institution’s norms.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The behavior can be shaped without conscious commitment to norms.

choices.text: The model must search the institution’s files every time.
choices.feedback: Close, but fine-tuning and retrieval are different. Retrieval may be added, but fine-tuned behavior can persist in weights or adapters.

questions.stem: A base model is adapted on thousands of institution-specific examples, then later answers new users in that style. What changed most directly?
questions.correct_choice_text: The adapted model’s weights or adapter behavior can carry that pattern into later inference runs.

choices.text: Training can make some instruction-following or preference patterns more likely in later responses.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Fine-tuning can support alignment by durably shaping output patterns.

choices.text: Every future answer is guaranteed safe and true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth or safety.

choices.text: Only the visible system prompt changed for one session.
choices.feedback: Close, but a system prompt steers the current run. Fine-tuning is durable training.

choices.text: The model acquired human values and moral agency.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Fine-tuning changes output patterns; it does not create moral agency.

questions.stem: If fine-tuning is used for alignment, what does that mean about future model behavior?
questions.correct_choice_text: Training can make some instruction-following or preference patterns more likely in later responses.

cards.objective: Distinguish fine-tuning or adapter training as durable post-pretraining adaptation from prompt, RAG, and sampling-time steering.

cards.title: Alignment

choices.text: Its behavior was shaped toward instructions and safety boundaries, but that does not prove moral understanding.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Alignment shapes behavior; it is not magic morality.

choices.text: The model must understand morality the way a person does.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Refusal behavior can be shaped without human moral understanding.

choices.text: Every answer from the assistant is now guaranteed true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Alignment can shape behavior, but it does not guarantee factual truth.

choices.text: The LLM stopped using learned weights and became a rulebook.
choices.feedback: Close, but an aligned product can still use a learned model plus rules, filters, or policies.

questions.stem: An alignment-shaped assistant refuses a harmful request and follows a safer instruction instead. What should a model-literate learner conclude?
questions.correct_choice_text: Its behavior was shaped toward instructions and safety boundaries, but that does not prove moral understanding.

choices.text: Some alignment changes can be durable training changes, while other controls steer or filter the current run.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Alignment can happen through several layers, not one magic switch.

choices.text: Every alignment layer permanently rewrites the model weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning may change weights, but system prompts and filters can steer behavior at runtime.

choices.text: Alignment is only a better prompt typed by the user.
choices.feedback: Close, but alignment can include training, policies, evaluation, and runtime controls beyond the user prompt.

choices.text: Alignment is the same thing as sampling the most probable token.
choices.feedback: Sampling chooses a next token from probabilities. Alignment shapes or constrains behavior around that process.

questions.stem: A product uses instruction fine-tuning, a system prompt, and a policy filter around one LLM. Which alignment map is most useful?
questions.correct_choice_text: Some alignment changes can be durable training changes, while other controls steer or filter the current run.

choices.text: Because shaped behavior can still fail, drift, or create unexpected tradeoffs in new situations.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Alignment needs evaluation; it is not a one-time guarantee.

choices.text: To check whether the model has developed a conscience.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Evaluation checks behavior and outcomes, not inner conscience.

choices.text: Because aligned models no longer need human review.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Aligned systems can still need review, monitoring, and accountability.

choices.text: Because lower training loss alone proves the model is safe.
choices.feedback: Close, but loss alone is not a full safety or usefulness evaluation.

questions.stem: Why do teams evaluate aligned models with tests and human feedback after training or deployment?
questions.correct_choice_text: Because shaped behavior can still fail, drift, or create unexpected tradeoffs in new situations.

choices.text: A policy or filter layer changed the visible output, not necessarily the model weights.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Product-level controls can affect what users see without being a weight update.

choices.text: The model learned new moral values during that one response.
choices.feedback: A blocked output is not evidence of a new conscience or durable training.

choices.text: The user prompt permanently rewrote the safety behavior.
choices.feedback: Prompting can steer a run, but it usually does not update weights.

choices.text: The answer became guaranteed true because it was allowed through.
choices.feedback: A safety decision is not the same as fact verification.

questions.stem: A guardrail blocks one unsafe response before the user sees it. What changed most directly?
questions.correct_choice_text: A policy or filter layer changed the visible output, not necessarily the model weights.

cards.objective: Reason about alignment as behavior shaping through training, prompts, policies, filters, evaluation, and deployment controls without treating it as conscience or truth guarantee.

cards.title: Inference

choices.text: Temporary activations and hidden states change; learned weights usually stay fixed.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Inference uses the map without redrawing it.

choices.text: The learned weights change after every user prompt.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Ordinary inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: The training dataset changes to include the new prompt.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A prompt can be current context without joining the training dataset.

choices.text: The model’s conscious attention shifts to the user’s intent.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Inference computes temporary vectors; it is not conscious attention.

questions.stem: During ordinary inference, the current context enters the model. What changes temporarily, and what usually stays fixed?
questions.correct_choice_text: Temporary activations and hidden states change; learned weights usually stay fixed.

choices.text: Context enters a forward pass, temporary states form, logits are produced, and sampling can choose a token.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the live next-token path through inference.

choices.text: Example enters training, loss updates weights, and a future model checkpoint is saved.
choices.feedback: Close, but that is a training path. Inference uses existing weights to produce the next-token scores.

choices.text: Retriever searches outside documents, then the model permanently learns the snippets.
choices.feedback: Retrieval can add context before inference, but it does not by itself make the model permanently learn snippets.

choices.text: Tokenizer splits text and the answer appears without model layers.
choices.feedback: Tokenization prepares input, but inference still runs model layers to produce scores.

questions.stem: In one inference step, which trace best matches the model’s next-token path?
questions.correct_choice_text: Context enters a forward pass, temporary states form, logits are produced, and sampling can choose a token.

choices.text: The earlier line is still in the current context, so fixed weights can use it during inference.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Context can feel like memory while still being temporary input.

choices.text: The earlier line was written into the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Same-conversation context is temporary input, not necessarily a weight update.

choices.text: The chatbot fine-tuned itself on the earlier line.
choices.feedback: Close, but adaptation within a context window is not the same as fine-tuning.

choices.text: The model searched every document it was pretrained on.
choices.feedback: This describes search better than ordinary inference. The model can use visible context without searching training sources.

questions.stem: A chatbot remembers an earlier line in the same conversation and uses it in the next answer. What is the best model-level explanation?
questions.correct_choice_text: The earlier line is still in the current context, so fixed weights can use it during inference.

choices.text: A useful answer now does not automatically teach the base model to behave differently tomorrow.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Inference can be useful without being durable training.

choices.text: Every good answer automatically becomes permanent skill.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Durable skill changes require training or another model-update process.

choices.text: The current prompt cannot affect the answer at all.
choices.feedback: Close, but fixed weights can still respond differently to different current contexts.

choices.text: There is no need to review outputs because inference is temporary.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Temporary outputs can still affect people and decisions, so review can matter.

questions.stem: If ordinary inference normally does not update weights, what follows for future conversations?
questions.correct_choice_text: A useful answer now does not automatically teach the base model to behave differently tomorrow.

cards.objective: Explain inference as a forward pass using fixed weights and temporary context-shaped states, not durable learning.

cards.title: Prompt vs Response

choices.text: Prompt tokens are given to the model; response tokens are generated and then appended to context.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Prompt is given; response is generated and appended.

choices.text: Both prompt and response tokens were typed by the user.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The user provides the prompt; the model generates response tokens.

choices.text: The model writes the whole response at once after reading the prompt.
choices.feedback: Close, but the model generates response tokens step by step.

choices.text: Each response token permanently updates the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Response tokens can shape the current run without updating weights.

questions.stem: A user gives a complete prompt, then the model begins writing response tokens. Which boundary matters most?
questions.correct_choice_text: Prompt tokens are given to the model; response tokens are generated and then appended to context.

choices.text: The original prompt plus the response so far, including the newly appended token.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The context grows as generated response tokens are appended.

choices.text: Only the newly chosen token, with the prompt removed.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The next step can use the prompt and response-so-far that remain in context.

choices.text: A permanent memory of the token stored in model weights.
choices.feedback: Close, but using a token later in the run is context, not a weight update.

choices.text: No context, because softmax disappears after one token.
choices.feedback: Softmax can help choose a token, but the chosen token can still be appended to the next context.

questions.stem: After the model chooses one next response token, what does the next inference step see?
questions.correct_choice_text: The original prompt plus the response so far, including the newly appended token.

choices.text: The current prompt/context changed, so the next response tokens were generated from better input.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Better context can improve the current response without training the model.

choices.text: The model was fine-tuned by the clarifying sentence.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A clarifying sentence can steer the current run; fine-tuning is additional training.

choices.text: The model understood its mistake like a person and decided to improve.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Better output can come from better context.

choices.text: Prompts cannot affect generated response tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but fixed weights still process different prompts into different response probabilities.

questions.stem: A generated response improves after the user adds a clarifying sentence. What changed most directly?
questions.correct_choice_text: The current prompt/context changed, so the next response tokens were generated from better input.

cards.objective: Separate given prompt/context tokens from generated response tokens and explain that generated tokens are appended into the next inference context.

cards.title: Tokenization

choices.text: Tokens can be uneven text chunks, including word pieces and punctuation.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Tokens are model-readable chunks, not always whole words.

choices.text: Every token must be a complete dictionary word.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Tokenizers can split words, punctuation, spaces, or other chunks.

choices.text: Each token is a permanent memory stored in the model.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A token is a representation chunk, not memory by itself.

choices.text: Each token is exactly one human concept.
choices.feedback: Close, but token boundaries do not perfectly match human concepts or meanings.

questions.stem: A tokenizer splits “startled.” into pieces like “start”, “led”, and “.” What should that tell a learner?
questions.correct_choice_text: Tokens can be uneven text chunks, including word pieces and punctuation.

choices.text: Text is split into tokens, tokens map to token IDs, and IDs look up embeddings.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Tokenization starts the bridge from text to numerical computation.

choices.text: Raw English sentences move through every layer without numerical representation.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The model needs numerical representations, starting with tokens and IDs.

choices.text: Weights are rewritten before text becomes tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but tokenization prepares the input; it does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: The model generates the finished response before tokenization happens.
choices.feedback: Generation also uses tokens. Text needs token representation before model layers can process it.

questions.stem: Before transformer layers process text, which tokens-to-IDs-to-embeddings path comes first?
questions.correct_choice_text: Text is split into tokens, tokens map to token IDs, and IDs look up embeddings.

choices.text: Prompt text and generated response text can both be represented as tokens.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Tokenization applies to text entering and leaving the generation loop.

choices.text: Only prompt text becomes tokens; response text skips token IDs.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Generated response tokens are token IDs before being displayed as text.

choices.text: Only response text becomes tokens; prompts stay as raw English.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompts also need token representation before model processing.

choices.text: Tokenizing either one permanently trains the model.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Tokenization represents text; it does not update weights.

questions.stem: Both the user prompt and the generated response eventually appear as text. How does tokenization treat them?
questions.correct_choice_text: Prompt text and generated response text can both be represented as tokens.

cards.objective: Explain tokenization as turning text into model-readable chunks that may be words, word pieces, punctuation, or other units before ID lookup.

cards.title: Token IDs

choices.text: To look up a learned starting vector in the embedding table.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The ID is a lookup key for an embedding vector.

choices.text: To store the complete meaning of dog inside the number itself.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The number itself is not the meaning; it points to learned numerical patterns.

choices.text: To permanently remember this user’s sentence.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Token IDs represent chunks; they do not store a user memory.

choices.text: To skip embeddings and tensors entirely.
choices.feedback: Close, but the ID is a lookup key; embeddings and tensors still carry numerical representation forward.

questions.stem: After tokenization, the token “dog” maps to an integer ID. What does the model use that ID for next?
questions.correct_choice_text: To look up a learned starting vector in the embedding table.

choices.text: 982 is a lookup number for a token; meaning comes from learned patterns around embeddings and model layers.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The ID points into the system; it is not understanding by itself.

choices.text: 982 means cat because the model consciously understands the number.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. A lookup number does not imply conscious understanding.

choices.text: 982 guarantees every sentence about cats will be true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Token IDs do not guarantee factual output.

choices.text: 982 is randomly redefined every time the user sends a prompt.
choices.feedback: Close, but token ID mappings are fixed for a tokenizer/model setup; sampling randomness is a different step.

questions.stem: A learner says “982 means cat because the ID is the meaning.” What correction is most model- literate?
questions.correct_choice_text: 982 is a lookup number for a token; meaning comes from learned patterns around embeddings and model layers.

choices.text: The chosen ID can be displayed as text and appended as a response token in the current context.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generated tokens are IDs before they become visible text.

choices.text: The chosen ID permanently updates the model weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The chosen token can affect the next context without changing weights.

choices.text: The model skips IDs and chooses only raw human words.
choices.feedback: Close, but the model works with token IDs before text is displayed.

choices.text: The ID retrieves the original training document for that word.
choices.feedback: This describes retrieval better than token ID lookup. Token IDs point to token representations, not original documents.

questions.stem: During generation, the model chooses a next response token ID before the user sees text. What happens after that?
questions.correct_choice_text: The chosen ID can be displayed as text and appended as a response token in the current context.

cards.objective: Explain token IDs as lookup keys from token chunks to embedding rows, not meanings, memories, or source retrieval by themselves.

cards.title: Embeddings

choices.text: A learned starting vector from the embedding table.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The embedding is the token ID's learned numerical starting point.

choices.text: The temporary hidden state after attention has read the sentence.
choices.feedback: That describes a later context-shaped vector, not the starting lookup.

choices.text: The integer ID itself, before any lookup happens.
choices.feedback: The ID is a lookup key, not the vector returned by the table.

choices.text: A human-readable dictionary definition stored in English.
choices.feedback: Embeddings are numerical vectors, not text definitions.

questions.stem: The token ID for "dog" looks up a vector before the sentence context is processed. What is that vector?
questions.correct_choice_text: A learned starting vector from the embedding table.

choices.text: Context and layers reshape it into different temporary hidden states.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Embeddings start the token; hidden states are shaped by context during inference.

choices.text: The embedding table rewrites itself during ordinary inference.
choices.feedback: Ordinary inference usually uses fixed learned weights.

choices.text: The token ID changes meaning by becoming a new integer.
choices.feedback: The ID remains a lookup key; later vectors carry contextual information.

choices.text: The model stores a permanent memory of each sentence.
choices.feedback: Temporary hidden states are not permanent memories.

questions.stem: The same token starts with one embedding but appears in two different sentences. What can make its later internal vector differ?
questions.correct_choice_text: Context and layers reshape it into different temporary hidden states.

cards.objective: Distinguish an embedding as a learned starting vector retrieved from a durable embedding table from a temporary hidden state shaped by context.

cards.title: Vectors

choices.text: It is an ordered numerical representation the model can compute with.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Vectors let the model carry many numerical features at once.

choices.text: It is a single integer lookup key.
choices.feedback: That describes a token ID, not a vector of many values.

choices.text: It is a shaped block with several axes such as tokens and features.
choices.feedback: That is closer to a tensor; a vector is one-dimensional.

choices.text: It is the visible word shown to the user.
choices.feedback: Visible text is converted before the model computes with vectors.

questions.stem: A learner sees a long row of numbers representing a token. What makes it a vector in the model trace?
questions.correct_choice_text: It is an ordered numerical representation the model can compute with.

choices.text: Features are distributed across many dimensions, not usually one clean human label per dimension.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Vector meaning is spread out and computed, not stored as neat labels.

choices.text: Each dimension is a named English category.
choices.feedback: That makes distributed features too literal.

choices.text: Each vector is a saved chat memory.
choices.feedback: A vector representation is not a stored conversation.

choices.text: Each vector is a complete citation to a source.
choices.feedback: Vectors do not carry source citations by themselves.

questions.stem: A vector dimension feels tempting to label as "humor" or "truth." What should the learner remember?
questions.correct_choice_text: Features are distributed across many dimensions, not usually one clean human label per dimension.

choices.text: One can be a durable learned parameter; the other is temporary during a model run.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Some vectors live in learned model data, while others are temporary activations.

choices.text: Both are permanent memories of the user conversation.
choices.feedback: Conversation memory is a different product feature, not what these vectors are.

choices.text: Both are token IDs before embedding lookup.
choices.feedback: A token ID is a scalar lookup key, not the vector itself.

choices.text: Both are visible words after generation.
choices.feedback: Visible text is the display side, not the internal vector state.

questions.stem: An embedding row and a hidden state can both be vectors. Which boundary matters?
questions.correct_choice_text: One can be a durable learned parameter; the other is temporary during a model run.

cards.objective: Explain vectors as numerical representations with distributed features and distinguish vectors from scalar IDs, tensors, and human-readable labels.

cards.title: Tensors

choices.text: It is a shaped numerical block with dimensions the model operations can use.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Tensors organize numbers so layers can process positions, features, batches, or heads.

choices.text: It is one token ID for one word piece.
choices.feedback: A token ID is a lookup key, not a shaped numerical block.

choices.text: It is a rule written by a programmer.
choices.feedback: A tensor is data the model computes over, not an if-then rule.

choices.text: It is a permanent memory of every prompt.
choices.feedback: Tensors can be temporary activations or learned parameters, not conversation memory by default.

questions.stem: A model processes many token vectors arranged by token position and feature dimension. What makes that structure a tensor?
questions.correct_choice_text: It is a shaped numerical block with dimensions the model operations can use.

choices.text: Weight tensors can be durable learned parameters; activation tensors are temporary run-time values.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Tensor names can describe shape, while durability depends on whether the tensor is a parameter or an activation.

choices.text: All tensors are temporary and vanish after every token.
choices.feedback: That ignores learned parameter tensors.

choices.text: All tensors are permanent and become model memory.
choices.feedback: That ignores temporary activation tensors.

choices.text: Tensors are only visible words arranged in rows.
choices.feedback: The model uses numerical arrays, not visible text tables.

questions.correct_choice_text: Weight tensors can be durable learned parameters; activation tensors are temporary run-time values.

cards.objective: Explain tensors as shaped numerical blocks that organize many token vectors and features for layer operations.

cards.title: Attention

choices.text: Weighting relevance between token positions in the current context.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Attention is weighted relevance between positions, not awareness.

choices.text: Giving the model conscious focus on the sentence.
choices.feedback: Attention here is a calculation, not human attention.

choices.text: Choosing the final next token from probabilities.
choices.feedback: Sampling happens later after scores become probabilities.

choices.text: Rewriting the model weights to remember the sentence.
choices.feedback: The attention pattern is temporary during inference.

questions.stem: In "The dog chased the ball because it bounced," the model must relate "it" to earlier tokens. What is attention doing?
questions.correct_choice_text: Weighting relevance between token positions in the current context.

choices.text: Learned attention weights are durable, but the attention pattern for one prompt is temporary.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. The current relevance pattern is made for this run and does not become stored memory by itself.

choices.text: Every attention link becomes a saved user memory.
choices.feedback: Temporary relevance patterns are not saved memory.

choices.text: Attention is the same as a retrieval system searching documents.
choices.feedback: Retrieval can add text to context; attention works inside the model over positions.

choices.text: Attention replaces the need for hidden states.
choices.feedback: Attention helps shape hidden states rather than replacing them.

questions.stem: A learner says attention is permanent memory because it links words. What is the better boundary?
questions.correct_choice_text: Learned attention weights are durable, but the attention pattern for one prompt is temporary.

choices.text: Attention mixes information across positions; the MLP reshapes features within each position.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The two operations help in different ways inside a layer.

choices.text: Attention reshapes features within one position while the MLP searches documents.
choices.feedback: That swaps the roles and adds retrieval where it does not belong.

choices.text: Attention chooses the next token while the MLP converts logits to probabilities.
choices.feedback: Next-token choice and softmax happen later in the Decision Room.

choices.text: Attention is human awareness while the MLP is moral judgment.
choices.feedback: Both are computations, not mental or moral faculties.

questions.correct_choice_text: Attention mixes information across positions; the MLP reshapes features within each position.

choices.text: Inside transformer layers, helping shape temporary hidden states from the current context.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Attention is one layer operation in the forward pass.

choices.text: Before tokenization, deciding how text should be split.
choices.feedback: Tokenization happens before vectors and layers.

choices.text: After sampling, permanently storing the answer.
choices.feedback: Attention is not post-response storage.

choices.text: Outside the model, searching every connected file.
choices.feedback: That describes retrieval, not attention inside layers.

questions.stem: After tokens become vectors and tensors, where does attention fit in the workday stage?
questions.correct_choice_text: Inside transformer layers, helping shape temporary hidden states from the current context.

cards.objective: Explain attention as temporary weighted relevance between token positions that shapes hidden states, not human awareness or final token choice by itself.

cards.title: MLP

choices.text: The MLP, using learned weights to transform features at each token position.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The MLP is per-position feature reshaping inside the layer.

choices.text: Attention, because attention is the per-token feature reshaper.
choices.feedback: Attention mainly handles relevance across positions.

choices.text: Sampling, because sampling rewrites features before softmax.
choices.feedback: Sampling chooses a token later; it does not reshape layer features.

choices.text: Retrieval, because retrieval searches private files for features.
choices.feedback: Retrieval is an outside-system step, not the MLP operation.

questions.stem: After attention has mixed information across token positions, a layer reshapes each position's feature vector. Which component is doing that work?
questions.correct_choice_text: The MLP, using learned weights to transform features at each token position.

choices.text: The learned MLP parameters persist; the activations for this prompt do not.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Durable weights and temporary activations are different parts of the same operation.

choices.text: Every activation becomes a new model weight.
choices.feedback: Ordinary inference does not turn activations into weight updates.

choices.text: The MLP has no learned parameters at all.

choices.text: The MLP stores the user prompt as permanent memory.
choices.feedback: Temporary activations are not user memory.

choices.text: Attention, because attention weights relevance between token positions.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pronoun-style cross-position relevance belongs to attention more than the MLP.

choices.text: Softmax, because softmax links words across the context.
choices.feedback: Softmax converts scores to probabilities later.

choices.text: Token IDs, because IDs compare sentence positions.
choices.feedback: IDs are lookup numbers, not cross-position relevance.

choices.text: Fine-tuning, because every pronoun link updates weights.

cards.objective: Explain the MLP as per-token feature reshaping inside each layer, distinct from attention mixing across token positions.

cards.title: Layers

choices.text: Temporary hidden states for each token position.

choices.text: The original training dataset.

choices.text: The user interface around the model.

choices.text: A permanent memory of the conversation.
choices.feedback: Hidden states are temporary unless another system stores information separately.

questions.stem: A transformer block repeats attention and MLP operations. What is being refined as the prompt moves through layers?

choices.text: Durable learned layer parameters are different from temporary layer activations.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. A layer contains learned weights, but each prompt creates temporary values.

choices.text: Every layer activation becomes durable training.
choices.feedback: Activations during inference usually do not update weights.

choices.text: Layer weights vanish after each response.
choices.feedback: Learned parameters are reused in future runs.

choices.text: Layers are only visible paragraphs in the answer.
choices.feedback: Layers are internal computations, not the displayed response.

questions.stem: Layer weights are reused across many prompts, while hidden states appear for one run. Which boundary matters?
questions.correct_choice_text: Durable learned layer parameters are different from temporary layer activations.

choices.text: Layers are repeated numerical transformations, not conscious reasoning steps.
choices.feedback: Good myth repair. Layer depth can support complex computation without implying human thought.

choices.text: Each layer is a separate conscious agent debating the answer.
choices.feedback: That adds minds where there are computations.

choices.text: Each layer performs a live web search.
choices.feedback: Retrieval is outside the layer stack unless a system adds it.

choices.text: They are retrieval indexes for outside documents.

choices.text: They permanently add user corrections into weights.

questions.correct_choice_text: They help useful signal carry forward while attention and MLP transformations repeat.

cards.objective: Explain transformer layers as repeated attention-plus-MLP transformations that refine temporary hidden states without implying human thought steps.

cards.title: Hidden States

choices.text: The state is a private source document.

cards.title: Grounding

choices.feedback: Impressive demos still need deployment accounting.

choices.feedback: Cost counting is a human governance practice.

questions.stem: How should Costs We Must Count shape benefit claims?
questions.correct_choice_text: Pair useful AI possibilities with what must be protected, redesigned, governed, or funded.

cards.objective: Identify costs across training, inference, infrastructure, data, labor, privacy, and institutional deployment rather than treating digital systems as cost-free.

cards.title: Risk vs Myth

choices.text: Private data may be exposed, retained, or used outside the intended context.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Privacy and data governance are real institutional risks.

choices.text: Softmax will steal files from the laptop by itself.
choices.feedback: Softmax converts scores to probabilities; it is not file access.

choices.text: The model became conscious of the student records.
choices.feedback: Risk does not require consciousness.

choices.text: The answer must be true because the data was private.
choices.feedback: Private input does not guarantee accurate output.

questions.stem: A staff member pastes student records into a third-party AI tool. Which risk is real?
questions.correct_choice_text: Private data may be exposed, retained, or used outside the intended context.

choices.text: What data the tool can access, retain, log, and send into context.
choices.feedback: Good practice. Tool permissions and data flows are concrete risks.

choices.text: Whether the LLM has secret moral agency.
choices.feedback: Risk assessment should start with system behavior and governance.

choices.text: Whether every search result becomes permanent model memory.
choices.feedback: Retention depends on system design and policy, not magic memory.

choices.text: Whether all risk is sci-fi doom.
choices.feedback: Practical risks can be immediate and ordinary.

questions.stem: A tool has permission to search a campus drive. What should risk literacy ask first?
questions.correct_choice_text: What data the tool can access, retain, log, and send into context.

choices.text: Hallucination is unsupported output; lying implies intent.
choices.feedback: Good myth repair. Real risks do not require treating the model as a person.

choices.text: Every unsupported answer proves the model wanted to deceive.
choices.feedback: That imports human motives into the mechanism.

choices.text: Every false answer is only harmless word choice.
choices.feedback: Unsupported output can still cause real harm.

choices.text: Every citation-looking answer is grounded.
choices.feedback: Citation format needs evidence checking.

questions.stem: What boundary separates hallucination risk from lying?
questions.correct_choice_text: Hallucination is unsupported output; lying implies intent.

choices.text: Name the concrete risk, reject the myth, and choose safeguards that match the system.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Model literacy reduces both panic and complacency.

choices.text: Amplify the myth because it makes people cautious.
choices.feedback: Fearmongering can hide the real controls people need.

choices.text: Dismiss the risk because the model is not conscious.
choices.feedback: Non-conscious systems can still create real harms.

choices.text: Assume a larger model removes all governance needs.
choices.feedback: Better models still need policy, evaluation, and review.

questions.stem: Which response is best when a risk is real but a myth is also circulating?
questions.correct_choice_text: Name the concrete risk, reject the myth, and choose safeguards that match the system.

cards.objective: Separate practical institutional risks from myths, focusing on privacy, bias, unsupported claims, overreliance, governance, and non-conscious models.

cards.title: Benefits Worth Taking Seriously

choices.text: AI can assist drafting, translation, search, and access when humans review important outputs.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Benefits can be real without becoming utopian claims.

choices.text: AI should replace human judgment wherever it is faster.
choices.feedback: Speed does not remove accountability or context.

choices.text: AI guarantees access and accuracy for every learner.
choices.feedback: Access can improve, but quality and bias still need review.

choices.text: AI benefits only count if the model is conscious.
choices.feedback: Usefulness does not require a mind.

questions.stem: Which Benefits Worth Taking Seriously claim is useful and bounded rather than hype?
questions.correct_choice_text: AI can assist drafting, translation, search, and access when humans review important outputs.

choices.text: Trained weights working with temporary context, tools, retrieval, and human review.
choices.feedback: Good mechanism. Benefits are often system-level, not just the base model alone.

choices.text: Only the prompt, with no learned model behavior.
choices.feedback: Prompts matter, but learned weights and tools also matter.

choices.text: Only durable fine-tuning after every user message.
choices.feedback: Most use does not retrain after each prompt.

choices.text: Only hallucinated confidence.
choices.feedback: Useful assistance should be checked and grounded when needed.

questions.stem: During use, where do many AI benefits come from?
questions.correct_choice_text: Trained weights working with temporary context, tools, retrieval, and human review.

choices.text: Taking benefits seriously does not require believing in imminent utopia.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The goal is balanced literacy, not hype.

choices.text: Any mention of benefit means ignoring cost.
choices.feedback: Benefits and costs should be considered together.

choices.text: Only fearful claims can be responsible.
choices.feedback: Responsible judgment can include useful applications.

choices.text: A benefit proves the answer is always true.
choices.feedback: Usefulness still needs evaluation and review.

questions.stem: What misconception does this benefits card avoid?
questions.correct_choice_text: Taking benefits seriously does not require believing in imminent utopia.

cards.objective: Name realistic AI benefits such as drafting, access, translation, summarization, and search while keeping limits, costs, and human review visible.

cards.title: Human-Centered AI

choices.text: Human judgment, accountability, context, and an appeal path.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Human-centered AI keeps people responsible for consequential decisions.

choices.text: The model should decide because it sounds confident.
choices.feedback: Confidence is not accountability.

choices.text: A guardrail should replace all human review.
choices.feedback: Guardrails can help, but they do not carry human responsibility.

choices.text: The prompt should give the model moral understanding.
choices.feedback: Prompting does not create conscience.

questions.stem: In Human-Centered AI, what should remain central in a high-stakes advising workflow even if AI drafts options?
questions.correct_choice_text: Human judgment, accountability, context, and an appeal path.

choices.text: Prompts steer current behavior but do not by themselves create accountability, governance, or moral understanding.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Human-centered design is a system and institution choice.

choices.text: Prompts always durably update the model weights.
choices.feedback: Prompting usually changes temporary context.

choices.text: Prompts automatically verify every source.
choices.feedback: Verification needs evidence and process.

choices.text: Prompts remove the need to understand users.
choices.feedback: That reverses the point of human-centered design.

questions.stem: Why can prompts alone not make a system human-centered?
questions.correct_choice_text: Prompts steer current behavior but do not by themselves create accountability, governance, or moral understanding.

choices.text: Powerful AI should not automatically get decision authority.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Capability does not settle accountability.

choices.text: If AI is powerful, it should decide alone.
choices.feedback: High stakes call for oversight, not blind delegation.

choices.text: If AI is not conscious, there are no human responsibilities.
choices.feedback: People remain responsible for system design and use.

choices.text: If AI uses RAG, every decision is grounded enough.
choices.feedback: Retrieval can help, but decisions still need judgment.

questions.stem: What misconception does human-centered AI correct?
questions.correct_choice_text: Powerful AI should not automatically get decision authority.

choices.text: It names the values and oversight that should guide tradeoffs.
choices.feedback: Good connection. Counting costs should lead to better choices, not paralysis.

choices.text: It says risk literacy is unnecessary once benefits are visible.
choices.feedback: Human-centered work uses risk literacy.

choices.text: It says all tradeoffs are solved by larger models.
choices.feedback: Model size does not replace governance.

choices.text: It says humans should never use AI tools.
choices.feedback: Human-centered use can include AI assistance with accountability.

questions.stem: How does human-centered AI connect to the prior costs and risks cards?
questions.correct_choice_text: It names the values and oversight that should guide tradeoffs.

cards.objective: Explain human-centered AI as keeping human judgment, accountability, oversight, appeal, and context at the center of AI-assisted decisions.

cards.title: Better AI Is a Choice

choices.text: Use retrieval, evaluation, and human review when the task needs evidence.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Better AI comes from design choices and review, not one magic fix.

choices.text: Use only a longer prompt and skip evaluation.
choices.feedback: A prompt can help, but evaluation and review still matter.

choices.text: Use a larger model and remove accountability.
choices.feedback: Scale does not replace governance.

choices.text: Turn off retrieval so the model relies only on fluency.
choices.feedback: Evidence-sensitive tasks often need grounded context.

questions.stem: A team wants fewer unsupported answers in a student-support bot. Which design choice helps without pretending risk disappears?
questions.correct_choice_text: Use retrieval, evaluation, and human review when the task needs evidence.

choices.text: Training data, fine-tuning, policies, evaluation standards, and incentives.
choices.feedback: Good breadth. Some choices affect models; others affect workflows and institutions.

choices.text: Only the final sampled token in one response.
choices.feedback: That is a temporary generation outcome.

choices.text: Only one user prompt in one chat.
choices.feedback: A prompt can steer one run but may not durably shape the system.

choices.text: Only the color of the interface.
choices.feedback: Interface matters for use, but this answer asks about durable behavior shaping.

questions.stem: Which choices can durably shape future AI behavior?
questions.correct_choice_text: Training data, fine-tuning, policies, evaluation standards, and incentives.

choices.text: Harms are not inevitable just because the technology is useful.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Institutions can make better or worse design and governance choices.

choices.text: Every harm is unavoidable, so review is pointless.
choices.feedback: Review and design can reduce harms.

choices.text: Every benefit proves the system is already responsible.
choices.feedback: Useful systems still need governance.

choices.text: Every choice happens inside softmax.
choices.feedback: Many choices are human, institutional, and design-level.

questions.stem: What misconception does "better AI is a choice" avoid?
questions.correct_choice_text: Harms are not inevitable just because the technology is useful.

choices.text: Through design, policy, governance, evaluation, data choices, and incentives.
choices.feedback: Good connection. Values need implementation details.

choices.text: By assuming the model will choose ethics on its own.
choices.feedback: Models do not carry human accountability.

choices.text: By hiding limitations so users feel confident.
choices.feedback: Human-centered design should make limits legible.

choices.text: By treating all feedback as automatic fine-tuning.
choices.feedback: Feedback needs process before it becomes training.

questions.stem: How do human-centered values become practical choices?
questions.correct_choice_text: Through design, policy, governance, evaluation, data choices, and incentives.

cards.objective: Show that better AI outcomes depend on design, governance, incentives, evaluation, data choices, and human review, not inevitability.

cards.title: Effective Prompting from Model Literacy

choices.text: The current context the model uses for this run.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Prompting is context design.

choices.text: The model weights for all future users.
choices.feedback: Prompting usually does not update weights.

choices.text: The truth of missing evidence.
choices.feedback: A prompt cannot create evidence that is not available.

choices.text: The retriever's access to every private file.
choices.feedback: Access depends on connected tools and permissions.

questions.stem: A prompt includes the task, audience, evidence, constraints, and output format. What does that improve?
questions.correct_choice_text: The current context the model uses for this run.

choices.text: It works by shaping input context, constraints, and examples for a probabilistic model.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Better prompts help the model use context; they do not cast spells.

choices.text: The exact phrase permanently teaches the model.
choices.feedback: Durable teaching requires training or adaptation.

choices.text: A clever phrase guarantees a true answer.
choices.feedback: Truth still depends on evidence and review.

choices.text: A clever phrase gives the model consciousness.
choices.feedback: Prompt responsiveness is not awareness.

questions.stem: Why is prompting not magic wording?
questions.correct_choice_text: It works by shaping input context, constraints, and examples for a probabilistic model.

choices.text: Ask for sources or connect retrieval; do not treat citation- shaped output as enough.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Prompting can request evidence, but systems need actual evidence to ground claims.

choices.text: The model will always search the web by itself.
choices.feedback: Search requires a connected tool or retrieval system.

choices.text: The model can fine-tune itself into knowing the source.
choices.feedback: Ordinary prompting is not self-training.

choices.text: A confident citation format proves grounding.
choices.feedback: Citation shape must be checked against real sources.

questions.stem: A user asks for a citation but provides no source and no retrieval tool is connected. What should model literacy suggest?
questions.correct_choice_text: Ask for sources or connect retrieval; do not treat citation- shaped output as enough.

choices.text: It improves the current run while keeping review, evidence, and accountability visible.
choices.feedback: Good synthesis. Individual practice helps, but it does not replace governance or judgment.

choices.text: It moves all responsibility from humans to the model.
choices.feedback: Humans remain responsible for use and decisions.

choices.text: It removes the need for risk and cost review.
choices.feedback: Better prompts do not erase deployment questions.

choices.text: It makes generated tokens permanent training data.
choices.feedback: Generated tokens are appended in context, not automatically trained into weights.

questions.stem: How does effective prompting fit with human responsibility?
questions.correct_choice_text: It improves the current run while keeping review, evidence, and accountability visible.

cards.objective: Use model literacy to prompt clearly: shape current context, provide evidence and constraints, and remember prompting is not magic, training, or truth guarantee.

cards.title: Model Literate Synthesis

choices.text: Prompt tokens become IDs and embeddings, layers shape hidden states, logits become probabilities, sampling picks and appends tokens.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The synthesis follows the model path without turning it into magic.

choices.text: The model reads the prompt consciously and writes the whole answer at once.
choices.feedback: That skips the next-token mechanism and adds awareness.

choices.text: The prompt immediately updates weights, then the answer is retrieved from memory.
choices.feedback: Prompting usually changes context, not durable weights.

choices.text: RAG, softmax, and grounding are all the same truth- checking step.
choices.feedback: Those are different parts of a system and generation process.

questions.stem: A learner traces one answer from prompt to output. Which trace keeps the mechanism clear?
questions.correct_choice_text: Prompt tokens become IDs and embeddings, layers shape hidden states, logits become probabilities, sampling picks and appends tokens.

choices.text: Weights are durable; context and hidden states are temporary; retrieved evidence is inserted context; response tokens are generated.
choices.feedback: Good boundary. Keeping these buckets separate prevents many myths.

choices.text: Everything useful becomes permanent memory after one answer.
choices.feedback: Usefulness during a run does not imply durable storage.

choices.text: Retrieved evidence is the same as fine-tuning.
choices.feedback: Retrieval supplies context; fine-tuning changes learned behavior.

choices.text: Generated response tokens prove the answer is true.
choices.feedback: Generated text still needs evidence and review.

questions.stem: A model-literate explanation separates durable, temporary, retrieved, and generated pieces. Which separation is strongest?
questions.correct_choice_text: Weights are durable; context and hidden states are temporary; retrieved evidence is inserted context; response tokens are generated.

choices.text: Look for grounding, sources, uncertainty, and human review rather than treating fluency as wisdom.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Model literacy turns demystification into better judgment.

choices.text: Assume fluent output equals understanding.
choices.feedback: Fluency is not wisdom or awareness.

choices.text: Assume hallucinations are always intentional lies.
choices.feedback: Unsupported output does not prove intent.

choices.text: Assume probability is the same as truth.
choices.feedback: High-probability tokens can still be unsupported.

questions.stem: In Model Literate Synthesis, what should learners do when fluency outruns support?
questions.correct_choice_text: Look for grounding, sources, uncertainty, and human review rather than treating fluency as wisdom.

choices.text: It follows one prompt across training history, inference mechanics, evidence, risks, costs, and better human use.
choices.feedback: Good connection. The Journey ties mechanism to responsible decisions.

choices.text: It says training and inference are the same step.
choices.feedback: The Journey separates durable learning from temporary inference.

choices.text: It says risks are all myths once mechanics are visible.
choices.feedback: Mechanics clarify real risks as well as myths.

choices.text: It says human judgment matters only before tokenization.
choices.feedback: Judgment matters throughout use and deployment.

questions.stem: How does the day-in-the-life through-line help final synthesis?
questions.correct_choice_text: It follows one prompt across training history, inference mechanics, evidence, risks, costs, and better human use.

choices.text: LLMs are powerful pattern systems whose outputs need evidence, context awareness, and human responsibility.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The badge marks durable model literacy, not trivia.

choices.text: LLMs are minds whose intentions explain every answer.
choices.feedback: Mind language can mislead when it hides the mechanism.

choices.text: LLMs are databases that always return stored facts.
choices.feedback: They generate tokens from weights and context, sometimes with retrieval.

choices.text: LLMs are harmless toys because myths are exaggerated.
choices.feedback: Reducing myths should sharpen attention to real risks.

questions.stem: What is the badge-level mental model Prompt Life wants to leave behind?
questions.correct_choice_text: LLMs are powerful pattern systems whose outputs need evidence, context awareness, and human responsibility.

cards.objective: Synthesize the full prompt-to-response path and use it to make better human judgments about evidence, limits, risks, benefits, and accountability.
